title: Evaluating Lossy Compression Rates of Deep Generative Models
speaker: Roger Grosse
abstract: Implicit generative models such as GANs have achieved remarkable progress at generating convincing fake images, but how well do they really match the distribution? Log-likelihood has been used extensively to evaluate generative models whenever it’s convenient to do so, but measuring log-likelihoods for implicit generative models presents computational challenges. Furthermore, in order to obtain a density, one needs to smooth the distribution using a noisy model (typically Gaussian), and this choice is hard to motivate.
tags: Workshop on New Directions in Optimization Statistics and Machine Learning

proof_stages.label: Need for distributional evaluation beyond samples
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates likelihood and compression-based evaluation by arguing that sample quality, inception/FID-style scores, and exact-support GAN likelihoods fail to measure whether a generative model captures the data distribution.

proof_stages.label: AIS likelihood estimation for Gaussianized implicit models
proof_stages.summary: After imposing a Gaussian observation model to make implicit models assign nonzero densities, annealed importance sampling with MCMC transitions is introduced as an unbiased partition-function estimator for the marginal likelihood p(x).

proof_stages.label: BDMC validation by reverse AIS
proof_stages.summary: The speaker derives stochastic lower and upper log-partition bounds, using reverse AIS initialized by exact posterior samples available for model-generated data, so that the BDMC gap estimates AIS error.

proof_stages.label: Likelihood experiments diagnose submode dropping
proof_stages.summary: Applying AIS and BDMC to MNIST VAEs, GANs, and GMMNs shows accurate likelihood estimates, little evidence of memorization, and a large GAN likelihood deficit explained by subtle submode dropping rather than merely missing entire digit classes.

proof_stages.label: Rate-distortion formulation of lossy evaluation
proof_stages.summary: The second part reframes evaluation as lossy compression by defining distortion, rate as KL divergence from posterior code distribution to prior, and the constrained rate-distortion function together with its separable Lagrangian form.

proof_stages.label: AIS traces and bounds RD curves
proof_stages.summary: The optimal RD posterior is shown to be proportional to p(z)exp(-beta d(x,f(z))), matching AIS intermediate distributions and yielding an AIS-computable upper bound on the rate-distortion curve.

proof_stages.label: Empirical RD-curve validation and comparisons
proof_stages.summary: The method is validated on tractable and deliberately damaged models, then used to compare VAEs, GANs, architectures, inference-network approximations, and distortion metrics, showing that full RD curves reveal distinctions hidden by scalar likelihoods.

examples.engagement_reason: The MNIST digit examples are analyzed via sample proportions, posterior reconstructions, a log-likelihood-loss calculation for class imbalance, and submode-dropping diagnostics.
